coredata.dc:title: Bioactive carbon dots direct the osteogenic differentiation of human bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yu
Zhang, Fan
Zhang, Jing
Shao, Dan
Wang, Yanan
Li, Shuang
Lv, Shuang
Chi, Guangfan
Zhang, Ming
Chen, Li
Liu, Jianguo
coredata.dc:description: Bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) have been the focus of bone regeneration due to their excellent osteogenic potential and abundant source. However, the high-cost and low-efficiency differentiation of BMSCs into functional osteoblasts limits their clinical application. It is desirable to develop bioactive materials to integrate efficient differentiation and traceable properties in a biocompatible manner for MSC-based therapy. In this study, a new kind of bioactive carbon dot (CD) was facilely fabricated through a one-step hydrothermal method from adenosine and aspirin. These bioactive CDs were cytocompatible and biosafe with the capability of long-term fluorescent tracking of human bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (hBMSCs). Notably, the presence of bioactive CDs triggered and directed a series of events that followed the temporal pattern of osteogenic differentiation through the promotion of osteogenic transcription and enhancement of matrix mineralization. Moreover, cells with bioactive CDs exhibited more effective osteogenic differentiation behavior than cells treated with either adenosine or aspirin alone. Overall, these findings clearly showed that adenosine and aspirin-based CDs can direct osteogenic differentiation of hBMSCs in the absence of any external osteoinductive factors. The unique properties of bioactive CDs could provide insight into their potential for achieving efficient and safe MSC-based therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells
Adenosine
Aspirin
Carbon dots
Osteogenic differentiation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity assay and ALP staining

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of AACDs and its directed osteogenic differentiation of hBMSCs.
Characterization of AACDs. (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images, (b) size distribution histogram, (c) UV–vis absorption spectra, (d) fluorescence emission spectra of AACDs.

Cytotoxicity and long-term imaging of AACDs. (a) The relative viability of hBMSCs after incubation with AACDs at various concentrations for 24, 48, 72, and 96 h. (b) Fluorescence images of hBMSCs treated with 50 μg/mL AACDs for 3 h. Blue fluorescence standed for the AACDs, and the lysosomes were located by LysoTracker Red. (c) FACS analysis and (d) representative fluorescence images of hBMSCs after treating AACDs during 7-day period. Scale bars represent 10 μm. These data represent three independent experiments and are expressed as the mean ± SD. *P < 0.05 compared to the control group. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

ALP staining of hBMSCs. (a) Images of ALP staining at day 21 after incubation with different materials. (b) ALP activity of hBMSCs at day 21 after incubation with different materials. These data represent three independent experiments and are expressed as the mean ± SD. *P < 0.05 compared to the control group, #P < 0.05 compared to the AA group.

ARS staining of hBMSCs. (a) Images of ARS staining after 21 days of incubation with different materials. (b) Mineralization quantification by dissolved stained mineral deposits in 10% (v/v) cetylpyridinium chloride. These data represent three independent experiments and are expressed as the mean ± SD. *P < 0.05 compared to the control group, #P < 0.05 compared to the AA group.

RT PCR analysis of the expression of the osteogenic marker genes (a) ALP, (b) RUNX2, (c) OCN, and (d) BSP in hBMSCs after 14 days of incubation with different materials. These data represent three independent experiments and are expressed as the mean ± SD. *P < 0.05 compared to the control group, #P < 0.05 compared to the AA group.

Hematology parameters after the exposure of AACDs for 28 days. Changes in (a) WBC, (b) RBC, (c) HCT, (d) MCV, (e) HB, (f) PLT, (g) MCH, and (h) MCH in control mice, AA-treated mice, and AACD-treated mice after 28 days of treatment. Each bar represents the mean ± SD (n = 8).

Serum biochemistry data after the exposure of AACDs for 28 days. Changes in (a) AST, (b) ALT, (c) ALP, (d) ALB, (e) BUN, (f) CREA, (g) TC, and (h) TG in control mice, AA-treated mice, and AACD-treated mice after 28 days of treatment. Each bar represents the mean ± SD (n = 8).
Representative H&E-stained images of main organs including liver, spleen, kidneys, heart, and lungs from mice of different groups. The scale bars represent 100 μm.

head.ce:title: Bioactive carbon dots direct the osteogenic differentiation of human bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Surgery Department, No. 1 Hospital of Jilin University, No. 71, Xinmin Street, Chaoyang District, Changchun, 130021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Basic Medical Sciences, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nursing, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Magnetic Resonance Imaging, Yinghe Medical Imaging And Diagnosis Center of Changchun Chinese Medicine University, Changchun, 130021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yinghe Medical Imaging And Diagnosis Center of Changchun Chinese Medicine University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Basic Medical Sciences, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) have been the focus of bone regeneration due to their excellent osteogenic potential and abundant source. However, the high-cost and low-efficiency differentiation of BMSCs into functional osteoblasts limits their clinical application. It is desirable to develop bioactive materials to integrate efficient differentiation and traceable properties in a biocompatible manner for MSC-based therapy. In this study, a new kind of bioactive carbon dot (CD) was facilely fabricated through a one-step hydrothermal method from adenosine and aspirin. These bioactive CDs were cytocompatible and biosafe with the capability of long-term fluorescent tracking of human bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (hBMSCs). Notably, the presence of bioactive CDs triggered and directed a series of events that followed the temporal pattern of osteogenic differentiation through the promotion of osteogenic transcription and enhancement of matrix mineralization. Moreover, cells with bioactive CDs exhibited more effective osteogenic differentiation behavior than cells treated with either adenosine or aspirin alone. Overall, these findings clearly showed that adenosine and aspirin-based CDs can direct osteogenic differentiation of hBMSCs in the absence of any external osteoinductive factors. The unique properties of bioactive CDs could provide insight into their potential for achieving efficient and safe MSC-based therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: The management of bone loss, osteanabrosis, and other conditions with poor bone formation presents a formidable challenge in the field of regenerative medicine [1,2]. The crucial role of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the initial formation and maintenance of bone makes MSCs a promising cell source for generating new bone in the presence of osteoinductive cues [3,4]. In particular, bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) have been the focus of most bone repair studies due to their excellent osteogenic potential and abundant source [5,6]. Despite these opportunities, the high-cost and low-efficiency differentiation of BMSCs into functional osteoblasts limits their application in the clinic [7]. Therefore, it is urgently necessary to develop reliable and effective approaches for directing and/or promoting osteogenic differentiation of BMSCs. Several small molecules have been widely explored for in vitro osteogenic differentiation with advantages including cost-effectiveness, non-immunogenicity, and easy manipulation and standardization [8–10]. To improve the efficacy of small molecules to direct osteogenic differentiation of BMSCs, an ideal strategy with combinational effects, higher retention, and traceable properties is desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging fluorescent nanomaterials with superior biocompatibility for biological research considering their excellent optical properties, chemical stability, and renal clearance ability [11–15]. Benefitting from its effective endocytosis and ease of modification, CDs have extensively contributed to achieving targeted drug delivery and fluorescent tracking of drug distribution [16–18]. In addition to long-term monitoring of stem cell differentiation, our group has shown that citric acid-based CDs are capable of promoting the osteogenic differentiation of MSCs through the ROS-mediated MAPK pathway [19]. To further manipulate its physical characteristics and chemical compositions, multifunctional CDs have been designed and fabricated recently by the sophisticated introduction of bioactive precursors, including small moleculs, nucleic acids, and proteins, into a bottom-up synthetic strategy [20,21]. Compared with traditional citric acid-based CDs, these bioactive CDs could have distinct pharmacological activities for extending their applications against cancer and pathogens [22,23]. In a subsequent study, we also demonstrated that bioactive CDs exhibit improved anti-cancer performance compared with free small molecules in both in vitro and in vivo studies [17]. However, bioactive CDs obtained from dual-functional precursors have yet to be established.

In this study, we used adenosine and aspirin as co-precursors to fabricate multifunctional CDs with desired biofunctions and biocompatibility. In this system, adenosine, a metabolite of ATP, has been shown to directly convert hPSCs into functional osteoblasts through the involvement of the adenosine tri-phosphate (ATP) A2b receptor (A2bR) [24,25]. Aspirin, a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that has been well known for decades, has been explored for its immunomodulatory effect on MSCs and for preventing bone loss in vivo [26,27]. Considering these findings, we propose that adenosine- and aspirin-based CDs (AACDs) could be effective and safe osteoinduction agents for deriving osteoblasts from BMSCs. As a proof-of-concept, we characterized and compared human bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (hBMSCs) cultured with these bioactive CDs or homologous molecular combinations and investigated the molecular mechanism for the combined effect on directing osteogenic differentiation.

Adenosine was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Aspirin was purchased from Solarbio Company (Beijing, China). Dexamethasone, l-ascorbic acid, β-glycerophosphate and cetylpyridinium chloride were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. DMEM/F-12 medium and fetal bovine serum (FBS, mesenchymal stem cell-qualified) were purchased from Gibco. LysoTracker Red, RIPA lysis buffer, a BCA assay kit, an alkaline phosphatase (ALP) staining kit and an ALP activity kit were achieved from the Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Jiangsu, China), and alizarin red (ARS) was obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China).

AACDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method through the condensation of aspirin and carbonization of adenosine. Aspirin was dissolved and dispersed in water due to ionization of COOH into COO with the assistance of adenosine. Briefly, 6.67 mg of aspirin was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water containing 19.8 mg of adenosine. The mixture was transferred into a 25 mL stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon liner (Tech Tai Beijing Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) and heated at 160 °C for 2 h. The solution when cooled at room temperature was dialyzed in deionized water to further purify the AACDs using a membrane (MWCO = 3 kD, Solarbio Company, Beijing, China) for 2 days, and every 3 h, the deionized water used for dialysis was changed to fresh deionized water. Finally, the transparent yellow-brown AACDs were lyophilized and collected for further use. HRTEM was obtained through a JEM-2100 F instrument. The size and zeta potential of the AACDs were characterized using a Nano-ZS 90 Nanosizer. FTIR spectra were measured using a Bruker model VECTOR22 Fourier transform spectrometer. XPS spectra data were collected using a VG ESCALAB MKII spectrometer with a Mg KR excitation (1253.6 eV). UV–vis absorption spectra were determined through a Shimadzu 3100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed using a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer.

According to the guidance of the Ethics Committee of the Jilin University Hospital, hBMSCs were isolated and purified from the normal human bone marrow of the donor by written informed consent. Adipogenic differentiation was identified, osteogenic differentiation was identified and hBMSCs surface markers were identified by flow cytometry. hBMSCs were maintained in normal medium (NM, DMEM/F-12 medium supplemented with 10% FBS, 10 ng/ml bFGF, 100 U/ml penicillin, and 100 μg/ml streptomycin) changing every 3 days. HBMSCs at passage 3 were used for the experimental studies.

The cytotoxicity of adenosine, aspirin, the AA mixture (the mass ratio of adenosine to aspirin was 3:1) and AACDs in the hBMSCs were determained using MTT assay. The assay was carried out in a 96-well plate containing 5 × 103 cells per well. For the following 24, 48, 72, and 96 h, the cells were cultured in complete medium containing different concentrations (from 3.125–200 μg/mL) of substances. Then replace the drug-containing medium with fresh medium containing 2.5 μg/mL MTT reagent. 4 h later 150 μl of DMSO was used to dissolve the formazan crystals in each well. The optical density was measured by a multifunctional microplate reader at 490 nm. The relative survival rate (mean (%) ± SD, n = 6) of the cells was calculated using the equation: survival rate (%) = (A490 treated sample/A490 untreated sample) × 100%.

To analyze the intracellular localization of AACDs, an assay was performed by a 6-well plate containing 5 × 104 cells per well. HBMSCs were cultured with AACDs (50 μg/mL) for 3 h. Before observing AACD locations by confocal laser scanning microscopy LysoTracker Red fluoroprobe for an additional 0.5 h. We conducted further research on the fluorescence properties of AACDs. AACDs (50 μg/mL) were added to a 24-well plate containing 1 × 104 cells/well. After the AACDs were cultured with the cells for 24 h, fresh medium was added to the wells. Intracellular fluorescence was observed by fluorescence microscopy at 3 h, 24 h and 7 days. Furthermore, the fluorescence signal intensity was quantitatively analyzed by flow cytometry after the cells were collected.

The osteogenic differentiation of hBMSCs was induced in osteogenic medium (OM, normal medium added with 0.1 μM dexamethasone, 50 μg/mL ascorbic acid, and 10 mM β-glycerophosphate) or normal medium (NM, without any osteoinductive factors). The cells in both OM and NM were treated with adenosine, aspirin, AA mixture and AACDs (50 μg/mL) respectively. hBMSCs were used at passage 3 for all experiments. The medium was changed every two days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity assay and ALP staining
ce:section.ce:para: For ALP activity assays, cells were harvested and lysed by RIPA lysis buffer. The supernatants were analyzed by an ALP activity kit together with a BCA assay kit. We determined ALP activity in each well and then normalized them to the total protein concentration in the same well. For ALP staining assay, cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde and washed with pure water three times, then processed with the ALP stain kit following the standard protocols.

For the mineralized matrix characterization, we fixed the cells with 4% paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 10–20 min at day 21. Then the cells were stained with 1% Alizarin red S solution for 20–30 min at room temperature. Quantification of mineralization based on measuring the optical density at 570 nm after the ARS deposit was dissolved in 10% (v/v) cetylpyridinium chloride.

We collected cells during the osteogenic differentiation of hBMSCs in normal media at 14 days. To determine the relative gene expression of osteoblasts-specific genes, total RNA is extracted and converted to cDNA, then qRT-PCR was performed. The expression of each investigated gene was normalized to the housekeeping gene GAPDH, and fold differences were calculated using the comparative Ct method. The osteogenic markers ALP, RUNX2, OCN, and BSP were analyzed.
ALP (forward primer: 5′TGCCTACTTGTGTGGCGTGAA3′, reverse primer: 5′TCACCCGAGTGGTAGTCACAATG3′);
RUNX2 (forward primer: 5′TTACCCCTCCTACCTGAGCC3′, reverse primer: 5′TGCCTGGGGTCTGAAAAAGG3′);
OCN (forward primer: 5′AGCAGCTTGGCCCAGACCTA3′, reverse primer: 5′TAGCGCCGGAGTCTGTTCACTAC3′);
BSP (forward primer: 5′AACGAAGAAAGCGAAGCAGAA3′, reverse primer: 5′TCTGCCTCTGTGCTGTTGGT3′);
GAPDH (forward primer: 5′CTTTGTCAAGCTCATTTCCTGG3′, reverse primer: 5′TCTTCCTCTTGTGCTCTTGC3′).

Male ICR mice (6–8 weeks old) were supplied by the Yisi Experimental Animal Technology (Changchun, China) and received care confirmed to the guidelines established by the Institute for Laboratory Animal Research, and the procedures were authorized by the Jilin University of China Animal Care and Use Committee. The animals were maintained in a specific pathogen-free (SPF) animal laboratory.

Eighteen male ICR mice (weighting 18–22 g) were randomly assigned into three groups (control, AA mixture and AACD). The mice were treated with PBS (control), AA-mixture (50 mg/kg i.v. administration), or AACDs (50 mg/kg i.v. administration). At 28 days after treatment, blood from each mouse was collected for hematology parameters and serum chemistry and analyses. The major organs (heart, liver, spleen, kidneys, and lungs) were collected for conventional histological analysis.

All the experiments were conducted at least three times, and the statistical significance (p < 0.05) was evaluated by the students’ t-test method when comparing only two groups. If more than two groups are compared, a one-way ANOVA analysis is used for evaluation, followed by Bonferroni’s post hoc test.

CDs were fabricated using adenosine and aspirin as co-precursors through a facile and green one-pot hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). In addition to their positive functions during MSC-mediated bone regeneration, the structure of these two small molecules aligned with the principle of a bottom-up synthetic strategy due to the amino group and carboxyl group of adenosine and aspirin, respectively. The morphology and structure of the as-synthesized AACDs were first determined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). As shown in Fig. 1
a and b, the AACDs possessed a uniform dispersion with particle diameters of 2–5 nm. A crystalline core containing a substantial amount of carbon with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.34 nm was observed in the AACDs and consisted of the (002) facet of graphite. The zeta potential of the AACDs was -9.8 mV (Supplementary Fig. S1). Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra of AACDs s showed the stretching vibration of OH at 3340 cm−1, CO at around 1648 cm−1 (Supplementary Fig. S2). Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed the presence of C, O, and N in AACDs (Supplementary Fig. S2). The C1s spectrum showed four types of carbon: CO at 288.6 eV, C–O at 286.8 eV, C–N at 285.2 eV and CC at 284.7, the O1s spectrum displayed two peaks of CO at 531.6 eV, and C–O at 532.8 eV. Collectively, these findings suggest the abundant −COOH and −OH on the surface of AACDs. We next sought to investigate the optical properties of AACDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the adenosine solution exhibited one classic absorption peak at a wavelength of 268 nm, while the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the aspirin solution possessed two absorption peaks at wavelengths of 230 and 278 nm, which corresponded to its π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. After hydrothermal processing, the AACDs possessed similar absorption spectra to aspirin, and there was one more absorption shoulder peak at a wavelength of 328 nm, indicating the surface state of the AACDs. Fig. 1 d shows the broad range of emission wavelengths of the AACDs, while the strongest fluorescence emission was observed at a wavelength of 410 nm under a 300 nm excitation wavelength. Notably, the AACD solutions could be stored at room temperature for as long as 90 days in the dark with little fluorescence loss and without forming any precipitate.

Our previous reports pointed out that citric acid-based CDs is cyto-compatibility to several cultured cells, even at concentrations up to 500 μg/mL [28]. To select the optimal dose for further analysis and osteogenic differentiation experiments, we determined the effect of adenosine, aspirin and AACDs on hBMSCs through an MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 2 a, there was no significant influence on cell viability after exposure to high concentrations of the AACDs at 100 μg/mL from 24 to 96 h, indicating superior biocompatibility with hBMSCs. However, both free adenosine and aspirin exhibited dose- and time-dependent cytotoxicity when the dose was greater than 50 μg/mL. As expected, the physical mixture of adenosine and aspirin in accordance with the synthetic procedure caused considerable cell death at high doses (Supplementary Fig. S4–6). Therefore, we used 50 μg/mL as the optimal dose of AACDs for the subsequent experiments.

Then, we investigated the internalization of AACDs on hBMSCs through confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 2b, the cells showed strong blue ﬂuorescence after incubation with AACDs for 3 h, and the overlay images with LysoTracker Red-labeled endosomes/lysosomes demonstrated the partial colocalization of the fluorescence between the AACDs and endosomes/lysosomes. This result suggested that some AACDs entered lysosomes, while others were found in the cytoplasm, which was in agreement with our previous results. In addition, consistent with our previous data, blue fluorescent signals were also observed in hBMSCs after prolonging the time from 3 h to 7 days (Fig. 2 c and d), which might benefit the in vivo monitoring the distribution and fate of MSCs during cell therapy. While fluorescent signal loss of AACDs in 7 days might be mainly might be attributed to the degradation-induced fluorescence quench and excretion-mediated intracellular fluorescence loss.

In summary, we prepared bioactive CDs through a facile hydrothermal treatment of adenosine and aspirin. The as-prepared AACDs were able to achieve tracking of hBMSCs through endocytosis without affecting cell viability, even at a dose of 100 μg/mL. Importantly, the presence of the AACDs not only facilitated the osteogenic differentiation efficiency of hBMSCs in the presence of osteogenic induction medium but also directed the osteogenic differentiation of hBMSCs in the absence of any external osteoinductive factors. The osteoinductive properties of the AACDs were partly attributed to the upregulated expression of osteoblast gene markers, including ALP, RUNX2, OCN, and BSP, and the resulting enhanced matrix mineralization. Moreover, cells treated with AACDs shown more effective osteogenic differentiation behavior compared with cells treated with a mixture of adenosine and aspirin. This study provided an efficient and safe method by introducing bioactive CDs for the osteogenic differentiation of hBMSCs, and these types of osteoinduction agents might find numerous biological applications in the fields of MSC-based cell therapy and bone regeneration.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is sponsored by the Jilin Science & Technology Development Plan (20190304029YY, 20180201025YY, 20170623062TC), and the Opening Project of the State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials of Jilin University under Grant No. SKLSSM 201713 and 201832. This work was conducted in Nanomedicine Engineering Laboratory of Jilin Province, Preclinical Pharmacology R&D Center of Jilin Province, Key Lab of Traditional Medicine for Diabetes of Jilin Province, and Key Lab of Pharmacology R&D of Jilin Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2019.03.035.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
